text: The following video demonstrates the technique of a robotic adrenalectomy with Firefly as a strategic approach to optimize key surgical steps of this operation. It is presented by the Department of Surgery from the University of Arizona College of Medicine and Banner University Medical Center in Phoenix, Arizona. This is a thirty-three year old obese female with an elevated serum aldosterone and aldosterone to renin ratio consistent with primary hyperaldosteronism. A preoperative adrenal vein sampling localized the increased aldosterone production to the right adrenal gland. She was therefore recommended to undergo a right adrenalectomy.

The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position with the bed flexed. The robotic trocars are placed in a linear fashion, approximately six centimeters apart, just below the costal margin. The camera is inserted into trocar three, while trocars two and four are used as main working ports. Trocar one is utilized for liver retraction. The robotic system is docked perpendicular to the bed over the patient's right side.

The surgeon prepares indocyanine green by mixing 25 milligrams with 10 cc's of saline for Firefly imaging. The procedure begins with the mobilization of the liver, dividing adhesions from a previous cholecystectomy. The right triangular ligament and retroperitoneal attachments are dissected until the inferior vena cava is visible. The liver is retracted cephalad using the fourth robotic arm, revealing the anterior surface of the adrenal gland.

The surgeon begins dissection of the adrenal gland by opening Girauda's fascia at the superior medial aspect of the kidney. The dissection progresses laterally, removing retroperitoneal fat from the superior aspect of the kidney. During this process, four cc's of endocyanin green solution is injected intravenously, which will be absorbed by the adrenal gland shortly.

The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to identify the inferior border of the adrenal gland, ensuring precise dissection without damaging the gland. Dissection proceeds posteriorly until the psoas muscle is visible, with retroperitoneal fat cleared and included with the adrenal gland specimen. The left hand retracts the adrenal gland and fat anteriorly, placing the fat under tension along the medial border to aid dissection. Girota's fascia is opened medially, facilitating the medial dissection of the adrenal gland, where arterial vessels are divided and the adrenal vein is identified. The robotic camera enhances visualization with a ten times zoom factor.

The surgeon uses Firefly imaging intermittently during the medial dissection to identify the border of the adrenal gland and check for the adrenal vein. The dissection progresses superiorly, clearing retroperitoneal fat from the psoas muscle. Small arterial vessels are divided with a vessel sealer. The medial dissection follows the inferior vena cava cephalad, with the wristed instrument in the right hand enhancing precision without significant manipulation of the inferior vena cava.

The Firefly imaging system highlights the adrenal vein, facilitating the careful dissection of retroperitoneal fat at the gland's medial aspect. The adrenal vein is visualized directly, circumferentially dissected with a vessel sealer, clipped, and divided. Wristed instruments are used to achieve optimal angles for clipping and dividing the vein.

The surgeon continues dissection along the superior aspect of the adrenal gland, using Firefly to identify its superior border. The retroperitoneal fat is cleared from the psoas muscle laterally and from the superior aspect of the kidney inferiorly, extending the dissection to the level of the peritoneal reflection.

At the conclusion of the procedure, the psoas muscle and superior aspect of the kidney will be cleared of retroperitoneal fat. Laterally, this will extend to the peritoneal reflection and medially to the inferior vena cava. In summary, utilization of Firefly for robotic adrenalectomy allows for better visualization of the border of the adrenal gland throughout the procedure, allowing the surgeon to avoid dissecting into the gland. It also aids in identification of the adrenal vein. The use of robotics for this case improves the overall visualization, and the wristed instruments improve the ease of dissection without significant tissue manipulation. Robotic adrenalectomy with Firefly is a useful technique to enhance visualization and facilitate a safe and clean dissection of the adrenal gland. Thank you.
